subUnits.title: 10.1 Economic Systems: Capitalism, Socialism, and Mixed Economy
notes: • Every society faces three fundamental economic questions:
• These arise from scarcity: unlimited wants vs limited resources
ASCII Diagram: The Three Basic Economic Questions
• Definition: The method a society uses to allocate scarce resources
• Definition: Economic system where private individuals own resources
- Individuals and firms own land, capital, enterprises
- Right to use, sell, or transfer property
- Example: Private ownership of businesses in Nigeria
- Primary incentive for production and investment
- Example: Nigerian entrepreneurs seeking business profits
- Prices determined by supply and demand
- Example: Rice prices rising when demand exceeds supply
- Consumers decide what is produced through purchases
- Example: High demand for smartphones drives production
- Government provides public goods and regulates markets
- Example: CBN regulating banking but not directing lending
• Competition drives firms to minimize costs
• Example: Nigerian telecom competition improving services
• Example: Freedom to start SMEs in Nigeria
• Example: Fintech innovation in Nigerian banking
• Wide variety of goods and services
• Example: Multiple brands of rice, phones, etc. in markets
• Poor may lack access to basic needs
• Nigerian Context: Wide gap between rich and poor
• Example: Environmental degradation from unchecked industry
• Business cycles cause booms and recessions
• Unemployment and inflation can be severe
• Example: Economic downturns affecting Nigerian businesses
• Profit motive may ignore social welfare
• Essential services may be unaffordable for poor
• Example: High healthcare costs limiting access
• Definition: Economic system where state owns and controls resources
- Government owns land, capital, major industries
- Example: State-owned enterprises in some sectors
- Government decides what, how, and for whom to produce
- Example: Central planning in former Soviet Union
- Production aims to meet social needs, not profit
- Basic goods provided at low or no cost
- Government redistributes income to reduce inequality
- Example: Price controls on essential goods
- Government decides available goods and services
- Less variety than in market economies
- Example: Limited product choices in command economies
• Basic needs provided to all citizens
• Example: Universal healthcare in some socialist systems
• Example: State employment in public sector
• Production prioritizes social welfare over profit
• Example: Investment in education and infrastructure
• Central planning can direct resources to priorities
• Example: Industrialization plans in developing countries
• Lack of profit motive reduces innovation
• Example: Slow response to consumer needs in command economies
• Individuals have less choice in occupation, consumption
• Example: Shortages and surpluses in planned economies
• Political considerations may override economic logic
• Example: Misallocation of resources for political gain
• Definition: Economic system combining market and state elements
a) Coexistence of Private and Public Sectors:
- Private firms operate alongside state enterprises
- Government intervenes to correct market failures
- Example: Nigeria's mix of private banks and CBN
- Prices largely determined by supply and demand
- Government regulates to protect consumers, environment
- Example: NAFDAC regulating food and drug safety
- Example: Conditional cash transfer programmes in Nigeria
- Government invests in infrastructure, key industries
- Example: Power sector reforms, agricultural subsidies
- Dominant in agriculture, trade, services, manufacturing
- Examples: Dangote Group, MTN, local markets, SMEs
- Key in oil/gas (NNPC), power (TCN), education, health
- Public Investment: Roads, railways, power infrastructure
• Definition: Using resources to maximize societal welfare
- Example: Nigerian firms adopting automation to reduce costs
- Example: Investment in sectors with highest returns
- Example: Nigerian fintech adapting to digital economy
• Poor power supply increases production costs
• Example: Manufacturers relying on expensive generators
• Corruption diverts resources from productive uses
• Example: Land disputes delaying development projects
• Education system not aligned with labour market needs
• Shortage of technical and vocational skills
• Example: Graduate unemployment alongside skills shortages
• Frequent policy changes create investment risks
• Example: Changes in import policies affecting businesses
• Power, transport, digital infrastructure reduce costs
• Example: Rail projects improving goods movement
• Example: EFCC efforts to combat corruption
• Align education with labour market needs
• Invest in technical and vocational training
• Example: N-Power programme for youth skills
• Example: Engaging private sector in policy formulation
• Every society must answer the three basic economic questions
• Economic systems differ in how they answer these questions
• Capitalism emphasizes efficiency and freedom; socialism emphasizes equity
• Mixed economies like Nigeria's seek to balance efficiency with equity
• Improving resource use efficiency is key to Nigeria's development
• Understanding economic systems helps in policy analysis and business strategy
diagramDescriptions: Diagram showing the three basic economic questions: What, How, For whom
Comparison chart of capitalism, socialism, and mixed economy features
Graph showing efficiency types: productive, allocative, dynamic
Nigerian context: Mixed economy structure with public and private sectors
Policy framework: Improving resource use efficiency in Nigeria
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Economic systems capitalism socialism mixed economy SSS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The three basic economic questions are:
quiz.explanation: Every society must decide what to produce, how to produce it, and for whom to produce it due to scarcity.

quiz.q: In capitalism, resources are primarily allocated by:
quiz.explanation: In capitalism, prices determined by supply and demand guide resource allocation through the market mechanism.

quiz.q: A key feature of socialism is:
quiz.explanation: Socialism is characterized by state ownership of resources and central planning of economic activity.
Mixed economies like Nigeria's combine market-based allocation with government intervention to correct market failures.
Competition in capitalism drives firms to minimize costs and innovate, promoting economic efficiency.
Central planning in socialism can lead to limited variety of goods and slower innovation due to lack of profit incentives.

quiz.q: In Nigeria's mixed economy, the private sector is dominant in:
quiz.explanation: While government controls key sectors like oil, the private sector dominates agriculture, trade, and most services in Nigeria.

opts: Producing at the lowest possible cost
quiz.explanation: Productive efficiency is achieved when goods are produced at the minimum possible cost using best available technology.

quiz.q: A barrier to resource efficiency in Nigeria is:
opts: Poor power supply increasing production costs
quiz.explanation: Inadequate power supply forces businesses to use expensive generators, raising production costs and reducing efficiency.

quiz.q: Improving resource use efficiency in Nigeria requires:
opts: Infrastructure investment, institutional reforms, and human capital development
quiz.explanation: Efficiency improvements need multi-faceted approach: better infrastructure, stronger institutions, and skilled workforce.
